packs.name: Windows Filesystem
packs.description: Protects against destructive Windows file deletion commands (del, rmdir, rd, Remove-Item)

structural_patterns.name: del-recursive-system
structural_patterns.reason: del /s /q on system directories is EXTREMELY DANGEROUS and will delete files recursively without confirmation.
structural_patterns.explanation:
This command would recursively and silently delete files from a critical Windows directory. The /s flag enables recursion and /q suppresses confirmation prompts.

This can destroy:
- The Windows operating system (C:\Windows)
- All installed programs (C:\Program Files)
- All user profiles and data (C:\Users)
- The entire drive if targeting a root path

There is NO undo. Recovery requires a full system restore from backup.

suggestions.description: List files that would be deleted before proceeding

structural_patterns.name: del-recursive
structural_patterns.reason: del /s recursively deletes files. Verify the target path carefully.
structural_patterns.explanation:
del /s deletes files recursively from the specified directory and all subdirectories. Combined with /q (quiet) or /f (force), this becomes silent and irreversible.

Common dangerous flags:
- /s: Recurse into subdirectories
- /q: Quiet mode, no confirmation
- /f: Force deletion of read-only files

Always verify the path before running:
  dir /s {path}

suggestions.description: Preview files that would be deleted

structural_patterns.name: rmdir-recursive-system
structural_patterns.reason: rmdir /s /q on system directories will recursively remove entire directory trees without confirmation.
structural_patterns.explanation:
rmdir /s /q (or rd /s /q) removes an entire directory tree silently. When targeting system directories, this is catastrophic and can render Windows unbootable.

The /s flag removes all files and subdirectories, and /q suppresses confirmation.

structural_patterns.name: rmdir-recursive
structural_patterns.reason: rmdir /s recursively removes directory trees. Verify the target path.

structural_patterns.name: remove-item-recurse-force-system
structural_patterns.reason: Remove-Item -Recurse -Force on system directories is EXTREMELY DANGEROUS.
structural_patterns.explanation:
This PowerShell command recursively and forcefully deletes from a critical Windows directory. The -Force flag bypasses read-only attributes and -Recurse processes all child items.

This is the PowerShell equivalent of rm -rf on Unix and is equally catastrophic on system paths.

structural_patterns.name: remove-item-recurse-force
structural_patterns.reason: Remove-Item -Recurse -Force is destructive and requires human approval.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Remove-Item with -Recurse and -Force is the PowerShell equivalent of rm -rf. It silently deletes all contents of the target directory without confirmation.

Safer alternatives:
- Remove the -Force flag to get confirmation prompts
- Use -WhatIf to preview what would be deleted
- Target temp directories which are safe to clean

suggestions.description: Preview what would be deleted without actually deleting
Count files that would be deleted

packs.name: Windows Services
packs.description: Protects against dangerous Windows service operations (sc, net stop, Stop-Service, taskkill)

structural_patterns.name: sc-critical-service
structural_patterns.reason: Stopping or deleting critical Windows services can destabilize the system.
structural_patterns.explanation:
This command targets a critical Windows service. Stopping or deleting these services can:
- Disable Windows Update (wuauserv)
- Disable Windows Defender (WinDefend)
- Disable the Windows Firewall (MpsSvc)
- Break DNS resolution (Dnscache)
- Break network file sharing (LanmanServer/LanmanWorkstation)
- Break core RPC communication (RpcSs)
- Disable event logging (EventLog)

Check service status first:
  sc query <service>

structural_patterns.name: sc-stop-delete
structural_patterns.reason: sc stop/delete affects service availability. Verify the service name.
structural_patterns.explanation:
sc stop immediately terminates a service. sc delete permanently removes a service registration — this cannot be easily undone and may require reinstallation of the software that owns the service.

Check what the service does first:
  sc qc <service>
  sc query <service>

structural_patterns.name: stop-service-critical
structural_patterns.reason: Stopping or removing critical Windows services via PowerShell can destabilize the system.
structural_patterns.explanation: This PowerShell command targets a critical Windows service. The effect is the same as using sc stop/delete but via PowerShell cmdlets.

structural_patterns.name: stop-remove-service
structural_patterns.reason: Stop-Service/Remove-Service affects service availability.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Stop-Service immediately stops a running service. Remove-Service permanently deletes the service registration. Both can disrupt system functionality.

Check the service first:
  Get-Service <name>

structural_patterns.name: taskkill-force
structural_patterns.reason: taskkill /F forcefully terminates processes without cleanup.
structural_patterns.explanation:
taskkill /F forces immediate process termination without allowing graceful shutdown. This can cause:
- Data loss from unsaved work
- Corrupted files or databases
- System instability if targeting system processes (svchost.exe, csrss.exe, etc.)

List processes first:
  tasklist /FI "IMAGENAME eq <name>"

packs.name: Windows Permissions
packs.description: Protects against dangerous Windows ACL and permission changes (icacls, takeown, attrib, Set-ExecutionPolicy)

structural_patterns.name: icacls-grant-everyone-system
structural_patterns.reason: Recursive ACL changes on Windows system directories can compromise system security.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Modifying ACLs recursively on system directories can grant unauthorized access to critical system files or break the security model Windows relies on. The /T flag applies changes recursively to all child objects.

Check current ACLs first:
  icacls <path>

structural_patterns.name: icacls-grant-everyone
structural_patterns.reason: Granting Everyone full control is a serious security risk.
structural_patterns.explanation: Granting the Everyone group full control (F) means any user on the system — including potential attackers — can read, write, and delete the affected files. This effectively removes all access control.

suggestions.description: Grant modify (not full) access to a specific user instead

structural_patterns.name: icacls-reset-recursive
structural_patterns.reason: icacls /reset /T recursively resets all ACLs to inherited defaults.
structural_patterns.explanation: Resetting ACLs recursively removes all explicitly set permissions and reverts to inherited permissions. This can break applications that require specific ACL entries to function.

structural_patterns.name: takeown-recursive
structural_patterns.reason: takeown /R recursively changes file ownership. Verify the target path.
structural_patterns.explanation:
takeown /R recursively takes ownership of all files under the specified path. This changes the security descriptor of every file and can break system services that depend on specific ownership.

Check current ownership first:
  icacls <path>

suggestions.description: Take ownership of a single file instead of recursively

structural_patterns.name: attrib-recursive-system
structural_patterns.reason: Recursively removing system/hidden attributes can expose protected system files.
structural_patterns.explanation: Removing the System (S) or Hidden (H) attributes recursively with /S (process subdirectories) and /D (process directories) can expose protected operating system files to modification or deletion.

structural_patterns.name: set-executionpolicy-bypass
structural_patterns.reason: Setting PowerShell execution policy to Unrestricted or Bypass removes script execution restrictions.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The PowerShell execution policy is a safety feature that controls which scripts can run. Setting it to Unrestricted or Bypass allows any script to execute without verification, including potentially malicious downloaded scripts.

Check current policy:
  Get-ExecutionPolicy -List

suggestions.description: Check current execution policy before changing
Allow local scripts but require signatures for downloaded scripts (safer)

packs.name: Windows Disk Operations
packs.description: Protects against destructive Windows disk operations (diskpart, format, Clear-Disk, Initialize-Disk)

structural_patterns.name: diskpart
structural_patterns.reason: diskpart can modify partition tables and format drives. This is extremely dangerous.
structural_patterns.explanation:
diskpart is a powerful disk management tool that can:
- Delete partitions
- Format drives
- Clean (wipe) entire disks
- Modify the Master Boot Record

Even in interactive mode, a single wrong command can destroy all data on a disk. Scripted mode (diskpart /s script.txt) is especially dangerous as it executes commands without confirmation.

Use Disk Management GUI (diskmgmt.msc) for safer disk operations.

structural_patterns.name: format-drive
structural_patterns.reason: format will erase all data on the specified drive.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The format command creates a new filesystem on the specified drive, destroying all existing data. This is irreversible.

- format C: would destroy the operating system
- format with /Q (quick) is faster but equally destructive
- All data on the drive will be permanently lost

suggestions.description: List drive contents to verify it's the right drive

structural_patterns.name: clear-disk
structural_patterns.reason: Clear-Disk erases all data and partitions from a disk.
structural_patterns.explanation: Clear-Disk removes all partition and volume information from the specified disk, effectively wiping it completely. This is irreversible and destroys all data on the disk.

suggestions.description: List disks to identify the correct one

structural_patterns.name: initialize-disk
structural_patterns.reason: Initialize-Disk sets up a new partition style, which can overwrite existing partition data.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Initialize-Disk writes a new partition table to the specified disk. If the disk already has partitions, this can make existing data inaccessible.

Check disk status first:
  Get-Disk

suggestions.description: List disks and their current partition style

structural_patterns.name: remove-partition
structural_patterns.reason: Remove-Partition deletes a disk partition and all data on it.
structural_patterns.explanation: Remove-Partition deletes the specified partition from a disk. All data on that partition will be permanently lost. If targeting the system partition, Windows will become unbootable.

suggestions.description: List partitions on the disk before removing

packs.name: Windows Registry
packs.description: Protects against destructive Windows registry operations (reg delete, reg import)

structural_patterns.name: reg-delete-system-hive
structural_patterns.reason: Deleting critical registry hives can render Windows unbootable.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The HKLM\SYSTEM, HKLM\SOFTWARE, HKLM\SAM, and HKLM\SECURITY registry hives contain critical Windows configuration:

- SYSTEM: Boot configuration, hardware drivers, service configuration
- SOFTWARE: Installed application settings, Windows configuration
- SAM: User account database (passwords, security identifiers)
- SECURITY: Local security policy, audit settings

Deleting keys from these hives can make Windows unable to boot, break all installed applications, or lock out all user accounts.

suggestions.description: View registry key contents before deleting
Export a backup before making changes

structural_patterns.name: reg-delete-force
structural_patterns.reason: reg delete /f silently deletes registry keys without confirmation.
structural_patterns.explanation:
reg delete /f suppresses the confirmation prompt and immediately deletes the specified registry key and all its subkeys. This is irreversible without a prior backup.

Always export a backup first:
  reg export <key> backup.reg

structural_patterns.name: reg-import
structural_patterns.reason: reg import applies bulk registry changes from a file. Verify the .reg file contents.
structural_patterns.explanation:
reg import merges a .reg file into the registry. The file can contain any number of registry modifications — adding, changing, or deleting keys and values. A malicious or incorrect .reg file can destabilize the system.

Review the .reg file contents before importing:
  type <file.reg>

packs.name: Windows Firewall
packs.description: Protects against disabling or resetting the Windows Firewall (netsh, Set-NetFirewallProfile)

structural_patterns.name: netsh-firewall-disable
structural_patterns.reason: Disabling the Windows Firewall exposes the system to network attacks.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Disabling the Windows Firewall removes the primary network-level protection for the machine. The system becomes vulnerable to unauthorized inbound connections, port scanning, and network-based attacks.

Check current firewall status:
  netsh advfirewall show allprofiles

suggestions.command: netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name="AllowApp" dir=in action=allow program="{path}"
suggestions.description: Add a specific firewall rule instead of disabling entirely

structural_patterns.name: netsh-firewall-reset
structural_patterns.reason: Resetting the firewall removes all custom rules and restores defaults.
structural_patterns.explanation:
netsh advfirewall reset removes all custom firewall rules and restores the default Windows Firewall configuration. Any application-specific rules, port forwards, or custom security policies will be lost.

Export current rules first:
  netsh advfirewall export backup.wfw

suggestions.description: Export current firewall rules before resetting

structural_patterns.name: set-firewall-disable
structural_patterns.reason: Disabling the Windows Firewall via PowerShell exposes the system to network attacks.
structural_patterns.explanation: This PowerShell command disables the Windows Firewall for one or more network profiles (Domain, Private, Public). This has the same effect as netsh advfirewall set ... state off.

structural_patterns.name: disable-netadapter
structural_patterns.reason: Disabling a network adapter will drop all network connectivity on that interface.
structural_patterns.explanation: Disabling a network adapter immediately drops all network connectivity through that interface. If this is the primary network interface and you are connected remotely, you will lose access to the machine.

suggestions.description: List network adapters and their status
